moveAnnotations.annotation: We play the Polish Opening, an unconventional but solid setup that immediately stakes a claim on the long diagonal with our bishop. This move prepares to fianchetto our light-squared bishop to b2, where it will exert long-term pressure along the a1-h8 diagonal. While it looks offbeat, the Polish Opening has been employed by strong players and offers flexible piece placement—we're not committing to a standard pawn structure, giving us good practical chances against opponents unfamiliar with the resulting positions.

Black immediately controls the center with this symmetrical advance, which is the natural German Defense response to b4. By playing ...d5, Black establishes a presence in the middle and prevents us from easily gaining space with c4. This move is very solid and announces that Black won't allow us a free hand in the center, though it does commit the queen's pawn early.

Now we complete our fianchetto setup, planting the bishop on its ideal long diagonal where it pressures both a1-h8 and supports our control of light squares. The bishop on b2 is especially useful here because it's aimed at g7 (if Black fianchettoes) and supports potential expansion with moves like c4 or e3. This modest-looking move is the whole point of b4—we've established a solid positional setup with good piece coordination.

Black brings the queen out actively, which is somewhat aggressive but not unreasonable here since there are no immediate tactical threats. The queen on d6 centralizes Black's attacking piece and supports the d5 pawn while eyeing the long diagonal. However, posting the queen this early can sometimes become a liability if we find tactical opportunities, so we should look to develop our pieces (knight to f3, perhaps c4) and see if Black's queen placement becomes uncomfortable under pressure.
